abstract: While isomerism is a cornerstone of organic chemistry, it remains an elusive phenomenon within the field of covalent organic frameworks (COFs). In this work, we demonstrate the first controllable synthesis of topological isomers in three-dimensional COFs by utilizing a specific tetrahedral building unit under varying solvent conditions. Through this strategy, we isolated two distinct isomers, JUC-620 and JUC-621, which adopt **dia** and **qtz** topologies, respectively. Their structures were comprehensively characterized using powder X-ray diffraction and transmission electron microscopy. These isomeric frameworks exhibit strikingly different textural properties; specifically, the **qtz**-netted JUC-621 features permanent mesopores (~23 Å) and a high surface area (~2060 m² g⁻¹), significantly exceeding the microporous JUC-620 (~12 Å and 980 m² g⁻¹). Due to its superior porosity, JUC-621 demonstrates efficient dye removal and exceptional iodine uptake (6.7 g g⁻¹), a 2.3-fold increase over JUC-620. This study establishes a novel methodology for engineering COF isomers, thereby expanding the structural diversity and functional potential of crystalline porous materials.
